turns.content: I'm getting a panic in my code when running the tutorial example. The error says index out of bounds at position 5. I tried using vec::retain to filter items but it seems to be shrinking the vector while iterating, which crashes the loop.

That panic happens because you are mutating a collection while iterating over it in place. When you call retain on a Vec, the indices shift as elements are removed, so using those original indices causes an out of bounds access. Instead of tracking indices manually, you should simply use the iterator returned by retain to iterate safely without needing index arithmetic.

Okay, I see now that the manual indexing was the problem. But my next goal is to remove consecutive duplicates from a list of strings like ["a", "b", "a", "c", "a"] to get ["a", "b", "c", "a"]. I tried using dedup but it didn't work as expected because it only removes adjacent items.

You are correct that dedup only handles consecutive duplicates. For your use case where you want to remove all subsequent occurrences of the same element regardless of position, you need to sort the vector first and then apply dedup. The pattern is to sort by value using a custom sort key, then call dedup_by_key or just dedup after sorting if the order doesn't matter for uniqueness.
I'm still confused about which method is best. If I have ["a", "b", "a", "c", "a"] and I sort it, do I lose the original order? And should I use dedup_by or just regular dedup after sorting?

Sorting does change the order to alphabetical, but that is usually fine if you only care about unique values. Since your strings are already comparable lexicographically, you can simply sort the Vec and then use dedup to remove consecutive duplicates. This avoids needing a custom key function for simple string comparisons.

Wait, I think I misunderstood how dedup works after sorting. If I have ["a", "b", "a", "c", "a"] and sort it to get ["a", "a", "a", "b", "c"], then calling dedup will leave me with just ["a", "b", "c"]. Is that what I want? And does this work for other types like integers too?

Yes, exactly. After sorting ["a", "a", "a", "b", "c"] into place, dedup removes the second and third "a" leaving you with ["a", "b", "c"]. This same pattern works for any type that implements Ord like integers or floats, so you can use this sort-then-dedup approach universally for removing duplicates.
I'm trying to filter a Vec<i32> in Rust but I want to keep only even numbers, how do you do that?

You can use the `retain` method on your `Vec`. It takes a closure as an argument. For example: `vec.retain(|x| x % 2 == 0);`. This modifies the vector in place, keeping only elements that pass the predicate.
Okay, so if I want to remove all duplicate values from a Vec<i32>, which method should I use?
For removing consecutive duplicates, you can call `dedup()` on your vector. It removes adjacent duplicates in-place.
But wait, what if I want to remove all duplicates regardless of order? Should I sort first?
Yes! You can sort the vector using `sort()` or `sort_unstable()`, then call `dedup()` afterwards. This will remove all duplicates, not just consecutive ones.
